questions.question: How do I manage stress when I can't change my situation?
questions.answer: Focus on what you CAN control: (1) Your response — practice the 4-7-8 breathing technique to calm your nervous system. (2) Your boundaries — say 'no' to non-essential commitments. (3) Your self-care — protect sleep, exercise, and social connection. (4) Your perspective — reframe the situation as a challenge, not a threat. Accept what you can't change, but don't resign to it.

questions.question: What's the fastest way to reduce stress in the moment?
questions.answer: Physical movement. Go for a 10-minute walk, do 20 jumping jacks, or stretch. Movement completes the stress cycle by burning off stress hormones. If you can't move, try the 4-7-8 breathing technique: inhale 4 seconds, hold 7 seconds, exhale 8 seconds. Repeat 3-4 times. Both methods activate your parasympathetic nervous system (rest and digest).

questions.question: How do I know if I'm burned out vs just stressed?
questions.answer: Stress is having too much to do; burnout is feeling like nothing matters. Key signs of burnout: (1) Emotional exhaustion — you're depleted, not just tired. (2) Cynicism — you're detached from your work and colleagues. (3) Reduced efficacy — you're less productive despite working more. (4) Physical symptoms — chronic headaches, insomnia, digestive issues. If you have these for more than 2 weeks, seek professional help.

questions.question: How do I help a stressed team member?
questions.answer: Four steps: (1) Notice — pay attention to changes in behavior. (2) Ask — 'I've noticed you seem overwhelmed. How can I help?' (3) Listen — don't fix, just hear them. (4) Support — help them prioritize, delegate, or take time off. Don't dismiss their stress ('just relax') or compare it to yours ('I'm stressed too'). Your job is to support, not solve.

questions.question: How do I prevent stress from affecting my relationships?
questions.answer: Three strategies: (1) Communicate — tell your partner/friends 'I'm stressed about work, it's not about you.' (2) Set boundaries — don't bring work stress home. Create a transition ritual (change clothes, take a walk) between work and personal time. (3) Prioritize connection — schedule quality time with loved ones, even when you're busy. Relationships are your stress buffer — don't sacrifice them.
